title: Making a timber tool tote tray
subtitle: Build an open-topped portable timber tray for carrying hand tools
excerpt: How to make a simple open-top timber tool tote tray from 12 mm plywood or MDF, with a solid timber central handle divider. The classic site and workshop carrying tray for hand tools, levels, and fixings.
sourceNotes: Traditional workshop and site practice. City and Guilds Level 2 Carpentry. Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1905, public domain).

glossaryTerms.definition: The central vertical timber member of a tool tote tray that serves both as the carrying handle and as a divider between two open compartments. It is shaped at the top with a half-circle cutout for the hand grip. It spans the full internal width of the tray and is glued and screwed to both end panels for strength.

content.text: A tool tote tray is an open-topped box with a central

, built from plywood or MDF. It carries hand tools, a spirit level, fixings, and a drill without a bag. A 500 mm long tray holds the core kit for most home-repair jobs. It takes about an hour to make from offcuts and does not require a router or bench plane.

12 mm plywood or MDF: one piece 500 x 200 mm for the base, two pieces 200 x 130 mm for the short ends, two pieces 500 x 130 mm for the long sides. One piece of 18 to 20 mm solid timber (any species) 200 x 180 mm for the handle divider. 35 mm wood screws. PVA glue. A jigsaw or circular saw. A cordless drill. A sanding block. A pencil and tape measure.

Cut all panels to size using the circular saw or jigsaw, checking each piece for square with the combination square. Cut the handle divider to width (200 mm) and height (180 mm). Mark the hand grip cutout: a 90 mm diameter half-circle, centred on the top edge of the divider piece. Drill a 10 mm starter hole inside the waste area, then cut the curve cleanly with the jigsaw. Sand the inside of the hand grip cutout with 120-grit wrapped around a short length of dowel.

Assemble the box with glue and screws: the two long sides screw into the edges of the two short ends, sitting outside them. Drill pilot holes to avoid splitting the plywood edge. Apply PVA to all mating surfaces before screwing together. Check the box is square by measuring the diagonals; equal diagonals means square. Allow the glue to firm for 10 minutes, then screw the base onto the bottom edge of the assembled side-and-end frame.

Apply PVA to the bottom edge and both side edges of the handle divider. Fit it into the centre of the assembled box, sitting on the base and butting against both long sides. Screw through each long side into the edge of the divider (two screws per side), and through the base up into the bottom edge of the divider (two screws from below). The divider should stand plumb and rigid.

Knock back all sharp edges with 120-grit sandpaper. A tool tote is a working item and does not need a painted finish; a coat of Danish oil on the handle grip and the top edge of the sides adds protection and makes the timber easier to grip in a dusty hand.

attrs.cause: Panels not held square during assembly.
attrs.fix: Before the glue sets, check the diagonal measurements and push the longer diagonal corners inward until both diagonals are equal.
